problem_statement: A university library's preservation pipeline began producing PDF/A files that its archive rejects for missing embedded font structures after a routine image rebuild. The application source did not intentionally change, ordinary reading copies remain usable, and the rejection appears downstream of document assembly. Determine the supported cause and a controlled remedy using copied documents and isolated builds only.

observations.statement: The preservation sink rejected 318 of 327 PDF/A submissions between 12:40Z and 14:10Z with conformance findings that four expected embedded font objects were absent.
For thesis T-2026-0441, the font-subset stage emitted zero embedded font objects at 11:02:18Z even though assembly had registered four font resources seven seconds earlier.
The source package for T-2026-0441 contains four licensed embeddable fonts, and an independent font inspector reports intact tables and embedding permissions for each file.
observations.provenance: source package and independent font inspection report

observations.statement: Build inventories hold application revision preserve-63d1 and settings fingerprint cfg-17c8 constant, but the rebuilt image resolves the transitive font-subsetter package to 4.9.0 instead of 4.8.1.
observations.provenance: signed software inventories from healthy and affected images

observations.statement: Paired isolated builds differing only in font-subsetter 4.8.1 versus 4.9.0 produce respectively four versus zero embedded font objects for all 30 copied affected documents.

Running each package's bundled conformance fixture outside the library application yields an embedded font object with 4.8.1 and an empty font-object set with 4.9.0 under the package's new automatic-pruning default.
The same copied inputs pass when the prior signed dependency bundle is restored without changing application source, document bytes, or preservation settings.
All affected workers produce identical failing artifacts, and their operating-system, locale, CPU architecture, and font-directory fingerprints match the last healthy fleet.
Reading-copy generation bypasses the font-subset stage and succeeds for the same 318 documents, while object storage and archive transport health remain normal.
